title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Weather where you're going may not match where you are CRAIG MEDRED OUTDOORS Published: November 20, 2005 Last Modified: November 20, 2005 at 06:47 AM Fifty miles northwest of Anchorage and a world away at the Yentna Station Roadhouse, it is easy for owner Dan Gabryszak to hate the weather reporters on this city's television stations. With their Anchorage-centric forecasts, they can make or break his business, particularly in winter when nearly all the "tourist'' traffic flowing up the Susitna River ice comes from Southcentral snowmachine riders. Gabryszak saw it last spring when the weather turned warm and snow disappeared early in Alaska's largest city. In the wilderness only 50 miles from the urban sprawl, Gabryszak was sitting on excellent spring riding conditions -- long…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Tubeless tires deflate fear of fall BIKES: Kit's cost may be reason it hasn't caught on in Alaska. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: October 30, 2005 Last Modified: November 4, 2005 at 10:39 AM The loud pop, coming as it did on a 30 mph descent of Hillside Drive, was one of the scariest sounds a cyclist can hear. Catastrophic failure of a bicycle front tire at this speed usually triggers a whole string of sounds. First, there is the bang of the tire. Then comes the whomp of a body hitting pavement or the ground. And finally there is some pathetic human moaning or cursing. That the sound of this blowout was followed only by a loud whoosh as the bike was eased to the side of the road can be credited to one thing: Stan's No Tubes and Sealant. It's the best cycling investment I e…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Bears mobbing the Russian River? It just ain't natural Published: August 14, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 06:21 AM Trying to sort out what is worst about the mess with bears on the Russian River is not easy. First there is the matter of the grizzly sow gut-shot and left to die. Whether you love bears or hate them -- and there are Alaskans with both views -- this is wrong. All that separates us from the rest of the animals is that thing we call humanity, and humanity hinges on treating each other, and the other animals, with some degree of respect. Thus, when legitimate hunters shoot animals, they try to ensure the animals die as quickly and painlessly as possible. If a grizzly rips another bear's gut open and leaves it to die, that's one thing. Bears do that. But i…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Ecotourism company makes the Green List Alaska tour company wins prestigious Conde Nast award By MELISSA DEVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: November 6, 2005 Last Modified: November 6, 2005 at 04:10 AM GIRDWOOD -- When Kirk Hoessle of Alaska Wildland Adventures discovered his company had been chosen as the highest-ranking outdoor tour operator in the world by a high-end glossy travel magazine this fall, he thought, "Wow. That's cool." Then he got back to work. Others aren't quite so nonchalant. They say that Wildland Adventures' winning Conde Nast's 11th annual Green Award as ecotourism guide of the year says a lot about changes happening in Alaska's outdoor-travel industry. Today, people want more out of their vacations, local experts say. Alaska's greatest asset remains its outdoors…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Alaska highways more lethal than wild lands Published: July 3, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 06:11 AM PETERSON CREEK -- The mini-SUV coming head-on in the wrong lane of the Seward Highway was an stark reminder that Alaska's No. 1 form of outdoor recreation -- driving for pleasure -- is also its most dangerous. Bear attacks, because of their rarity and our inherent fear of the wild, grab headlines. Climbing accidents, because of their novelty, attract attention. Drownings, because of the sadness of someone going in search of summer pleasure only to find death, become sad tragedies. Yet of these, driving kills most Alaskans. It might sound trite to say that you're in far more danger of dying in an automobile accident on the way to the Russian River than you are of bei…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Senator, son and staffer use supply of luck in Denali SUMMIT ATTEMPT: Hollis French and team chose an unpopular route. By LISA DEMER Anchorage Daily News Published: August 22, 2005 Last Modified: August 26, 2005 at 04:50 AM Click to enlarge On a pleasant June day, Hollis French, his teenage son and a colleague set off from Wonder Lake on a climb to the top of Mount McKinley. They never made it. French, a lawyer and state senator from Anchorage, had twice reached McKinley's summit from the popular West Buttress route, including in 1987 with his father and his wife, Peggy. This time he was looking forward to the long stretch in the wilderness with his son, Chris, 16. No television. No friends calling. No skateboarding or any of the umpteen things that steal teenagers from parents. Two years …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: What's in a bead? Just an angler out for a good time By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: September 4, 2005 Last Modified: September 4, 2005 at 04:51 AM Are you one of those anglers who likes to fish, fish, fish and catch nothing? Doubtful. Do you have a child, spouse, relative, friend or acquaintance who likes to fish, fish, fish and catch nothing? Unlikely. Would "The Old Man and the Sea'' have become one of America's great novels if ole Santiago had gone out in the Gulf Stream and caught nothing? Of course not. The purpose of fishing -- not to mention its joy -- is wrapped up in catching fish. Even the holiest of fly anglers in the hair-jacket crowd recognize this, but they just can't seem to countenance the idea that what holds for them should apply to the great unwashed mas…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Safety-conscious travel helps bear country trekkers avoid the danger zone By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: May 15, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 06:06 AM For more than a decade, Timothy Treadwell played dangerous games with the bears of the Katmai coast and never suffered a scratch. On his summer forays to the North, the Californian did almost everything experts say is wrong: He got between sow grizzlies and cubs. He approached bears that had their hair up in a sign of distress. He even reached out and touched bears. Before he was finally killed by a bear, Treadwell proved -- if nothing else -- that the coastal grizzlies of Alaska are amazingly tolerant animals, not the deadly man killers that so often spark outbreaks of Alaska bearanoia. No one doubts bears are da…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Backcountry adventures easier with modern sleds Published: March 6, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 06:01 AM Mount Susitna is a snow-covered peak 4,396 feet high, and is said to be the highest mountain in the lower Susitna River Valley. Its southern summit is in the shape of a woman's head that has helped give the mountain the local name "Sleeping Lady.'' Close to the northern summit is the abandoned carcass of a dead Arctic Cat Wildcat 700. No one has explained what the snowmobile was seeking at that altitude. From Bethel, it is better than 500 miles east up the Kuskokwim River to McGrath, and then another 350 south along the Iditarod Trail to Big Lake. People have traveled this route for much of Alaska's human history. First came the Athabascans who followed the riv…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: To catch trout, have nose for salmon KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: July 28, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 07:26 AM Some of the best-looking trout water produced absolutely nothing earlier this week. Glassy pools with roiling heads and swift tail-outs resembled images from classic fly-fishing paintings, but many offered no fish. Veteran angler Paul Cyr was not surprised. Nor was he concerned. He waded upstream, methodically peering into each slick and eddy for a telltale sign. And when he finally found it 100 yards upstream of where he'd started, he stopped short like a bird dog on point, crouched slightly and started casting. The spot, a piece of water hardly larger than a dining room tabletop, seemed an unlikely choice. The current was fast, part of a sequence of rapids th…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Tiny beauties lure true anglers KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: August 25, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 07:28 AM KENAI MOUNTAINS -- I had a hunch the ptarmigan would be up high Monday afternoon, feeding on blueberries that grew where patches of alder and mountain hemlock gave way to alpine tundra. And as it turned out, I was right. From the 2,000-foot-high valley bottom, I climbed a thousand feet to the brush line and soon stumbled into a covey of birds. Shotgun ready, I followed them into a thicket of head-high hemlock -- evergreens with distinctive purple cones that make the air smell like gin. I was working through a tangle of limbs when a ptarmigan cackled and the covey flushed low on the downhill side. There was time for a quick swing, a hasty shot and then they were go…

summary: Weekend fishing forecast in all Southcentral waterways FISHING LOCATIONS MAP click for full map SUSITNA VALLEY LOCATION & FISH DETAILS RATING Matanuska Valley lakes Trout, grayling, char, landlocked salmon, pike Fishing should be hot as fish begin their fall feeding frenzies. title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Better headlamps brighten darkening skies Published: August 28, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 06:27 AM The remnants of the first fall storm to sweep Southcentral Alaska were gusting across the Anchorage Hillside when I left the house to get the dogs out of their kennels for an evening run. The start of the season of howling winds brewed in the turbulent North Pacific had arrived unusually early to denude the elderberry bushes and savage weaker trees. Branches and leaves littered the yard. Outside the weather was warm, but the eternal sunshine of the near-arctic summer was clearly giving way to the oncoming blackness of winter. Almost as an afterthought, I grabbed a headlamp to help light the way if we were late coming back down the trail that runs into a valley behi…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Nasty ol' pike are here to stay, so get over it CRAIG MEDRED OUTDOORS Published: May 22, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 06:07 AM At Cheney Lake last week, sportfisheries biologists with the Alaska Department of Fish and Game were skirmishing once again in the never-ending battle against Anchorage's northern pike. As usual, the biologists were driving up the body count, but the pike were still winning. The pike are always winning. The pike have nature and time on their side, which means there's really only one way for us to win this one: Surrender. Give up. Face reality and transform the scourge into a blessing. Look, unless we're willing to use poison -- the piscean version of a nuclear weapon -- we can't win this war, and nobody, but nobody, even wants to consider t…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: IFQs for halibut charters make no economic sense Published: April 10, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 06:03 AM In a perfect world, there would be no reason to question the decision by the big-business dominated North Pacific Fishery Management Council to integrate a bunch of mom-and-pop charter fishing operations into the council's system of halibut catch quotas. On paper, the council's individual fishing quotas, or IFQs, are a great idea. They could end the ugly and contentious Alaska fish wars that have raged between competing interests for decades. IFQs could take allocation out of the hands of organizations like the Alaska Board of Fisheries and let the market decide who gets to catch what. If halibut are more valuable to tourism than to commercial fishing, charte…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fortunate fishermen strike rush of surprise red salmon KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: August 18, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 07:28 AM STERLING -- In a surprise surge that delivered all the late-summer fishing action a few lucky anglers could handle, red salmon fresh from Cook Inlet stormed the Kenai River last weekend and early this week by the hundreds of thousands, roiling eddies and bending rods from Bing's Landing upriver to Thompson's Hole and beyond. That was good news for anglers who had space in their freezers or smokehouses for last-minute fillets. Even better, late-season reds continued to bust into the Kenai in big numbers early this week -- 82,645 passed state sonar counters Monday and Tuesday -- and fishing is expected to stay sizzling hot through the weekend.…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishing with beads is akin to laser-guided golf balls By RICHARD CHIAPPONE Special to the Daily News Published: September 4, 2005 Last Modified: September 4, 2005 at 04:51 AM Tired of trying to catch trout that don't want to be caught? Weary of learning to cast a fly and mend the line for a drag-free drift and all that fancy stuff? Well, boys and girls, have I got something great for you. Pegged beads! Yes! With pegged beads there is no longer any need to develop actual fishing skills! Now everyone can catch dozens of trout a day and never strain their brains or their casting arms one bit. With ineffective, old-fashioned trout fishing tactics, first you had to figure out what the fish were eating. Then you had to tie all sorts of yarn and tinsel and bird feathers and the belly fur of Tibet…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: More kings get past weir on Deshka River; biologists aren't worried Daily News staff Published: August 3, 2005 Last Modified: August 3, 2005 at 06:11 AM WASILLA -- Call them the ones that got away. For the seventh year in a row, more king salmon swam past the weir on the Deshka River than fisheries biologists had hoped. Just over 37,000 chinook have passed the weir on the Susitna River tributary with a few more still trickling in each day. The escapement goal for the river -- the number biologists consider ideal to maximize future returns -- is between 18,000 and 28,000 kings. Biologists weren't too concerned. While not ideal, too many fish doesn't necessarily mean poor runs in the future, said state sport fisheries biologist Richard Yanusz. Many other factors, including ocean survival and…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Russian River veteran: To catch reds, you've got to see them first Russian River veteran says if you want to catch reds, you've got to see them first By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: June 19, 2005 Last Modified: June 22, 2005 at 06:02 PM RUSSIAN RIVER -- For Rocky Barnette, the journey to Alaska's most popular salmon fishery begins at the far opposite end of the North American continent. Long before salmon frenzy grips the 49th state, the now 79-year-old Florida angler is plotting his annual adventure to the northland. First there are October reservations to be made in order to ensure a June campsite in the always popular and coveted Russian River Campground. Then comes the lengthy spring drive from Boca Raton in his 32-foot motor home. The trip begins before the first salmo…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Angling paradise: Seward has a line on halibut and salmon fishing By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: April 21, 2005 Last Modified: June 28, 2005 at 12:40 PM Off the stern of the charter boat Glacier Breeze, the peaks of the Aialik Peninsula jutted from the sea like the teeth of a giant salmon shark. Puffins, gulls, murres and auklets flew overhead and, in the distance, Bear Glacier could be seen winding out of the Kenai Mountains, pouring its icy heart into Resurrection Bay. In the center of it all, angler Cheryl Myers had barely dropped her herring bait over the gunwale when she suddenly cried out. An unseen force was trying to wrench the salmon rod from her hands, forcing the retiree from Apple Valley, Calif., to hold on as the tip bent sharply toward the water. Skipper Chad Wa…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Looks like a huge year for silvers KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: July 21, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 07:25 AM SEWARD -- By midafternoon Saturday, 12-year-old Natalie Marsh was clearly exhausted. She had been awake since 4:30 a.m., and fishing hard since 7 a.m. aboard the 25-foot charter boat Strike Zone. A gale blasting in from the Aleutians had kicked up the seas. Sharp winds and needling rains had reddened Natalie's cheeks. Yet rest was out of the question. My daughter was only one silver salmon short of her six-fish limit. And as far as she was concerned, long hours spent battling heaving seas and big, hard-running fish -- including a 30-pound king salmon that tested her grip and nearly spooled her reel -- were simply part of a young girl's first saltwater angling adv…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishing for kings is a minor affair CAMPBELL CREEK: Kids under 16 get a crack at local salmon. By DON HUNTER Anchorage Daily News Published: June 24, 2005 Last Modified: June 24, 2005 at 06:27 AM King salmon fishing on a long stretch of Campbell Creek between Dimond Boulevard and the Old Seward Highway will open to anglers under 16 years old this weekend only. Kids -- and kids only -- can take one king over 20 inches per day or up to 10 per day under 20 inches on Saturday and Sunday, said Matt Miller, an area management biologist with the state Department of Fish and Game. The limited sport fishery will be open from 6 a.m. to 10 p.m. both days. Kids can use bait. The young anglers won't need fishing licenses, but they will need a harvest card to record their catch. The cards are available …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Downhill course of Quest is far, far from easy Published: July 10, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 06:13 AM FORT SELKIRK, YUKON TERRITORY, CANADA -- Somewhere upriver in the twilight of the subarctic night, we found ourselves slipping downhill on the smooth surface of a giant, wilderness water slide. Jim Jager of Anchorage, a veteran of the Yukon River Quest, a 470-mile river paddle from Whitehorse to Dawson, had described this phenomenon before, but it had never seemed so real. Comprehending a river come to resemble a ski slope can be difficult. Yet here it was, a bunny slope of water. Then the picture got really weird. The wide, blue-green flow seemed to climb into the grass-covered bluffs of basalt ahead. That water can run uphill is a given. Put enough force behin…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Start children on casting; fish will come later By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: August 28, 2005 Last Modified: August 28, 2005 at 03:49 AM Kids don't really like to fish. They like to cast. They like to catch fish. But that quiet, contemplative activity known to so many adults as "fishing'' is just not the normal kid's cup of milk. Particularly the wee ones. It's an attention span thing. Kids don't have one, or they don't have much of one. So what's a parent to do? • No. 1: Take them where they can cast. Don't worry if there are fish there. Lots of kids can have fun casting for hours and never catch anything but lake weeds. Hookless lures work great. You don't have to worry about anyone getting hurt that way, and the kids think they're fishing. Kids can enjoy many casting t…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Woman combines mushing with autism drive TREATMENT: Group wants to bring funds and support to all who suffer disorder. By BECKY STOPPA Anchorage Daily News Published: November 2, 2005 Last Modified: November 2, 2005 at 07:30 AM WASILLA -- Trisha Kolegar doesn't back down from a challenge. She set her sights on running the Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race when she was just 5 years old. Over the next several years, she logged hundreds of miles and countless hours, running dog teams through the woods near her Ohio home, preparing for the Last Great Race. In 2000, Kolegar's work paid off: She completed the Iditarod on her first attempt, despite the fact that she suffered a broken neck on the Happy River run only a couple of days into the 1,100-mile journey. Today, the 35-year-old, an Alaskan since…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Catch silvers while fall remains at bay KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: September 4, 2005 Last Modified: September 4, 2005 at 04:30 AM From Resurrection Bay to the Susitna Valley, silver salmon have fueled sportfishing obsessions in Southcentral Alaska since early July. Yet even now, with fall in the air and Labor Day weekend upon us, anglers are discovering the ride's not quite over. September always marks the last, best days of the fishing season. Trout, char and grayling are more active, bolting down salmon eggs in the streams and prowling in the shallows of local lakes. Northern pike, as Susitna Valley swamps and sloughs cool, are also growing hungry. Meanwhile, outside the ports of Seward, Homer and Whittier, halibut are still being caught on those days when the fickle fall weathe…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Alaska sports digest: Weaver leads silver salmon derby Daily News staff Published: August 6, 2005 Last Modified: August 7, 2005 at 04:03 AM Anchorage's Rodney Weaver Jr. grabbed the Downtown Soup Kitchen Slam'n Salm'n Derby's first-day lead Friday in the pro class with his 11.15-pound fish caught in Ship Creek. Weaver reeled in his fish around 4 p.m., while second place angler Jioji Lino of Anchorage caught his 9.45-pounder earlier in the day. Derby board member Marty Turnbow said low tide will be a good tide for anglers this week. "The bulk have been coming in at low tide," he said. "The fishing is going to get better as the week progresses. "It's all about the tide." Today's low tide is at 3:44 p.m. The derby, sponsored by GCI and the Alaska Railroad, raises money for the Downtown Soup K…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Seeing the light in packing gear CRAIG MEDRED OUTDOORS Published: July 24, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 06:15 AM NEAR THE SOUTHERN EDGE OF THE ARCTIC ICE -- All night the wind blew strong from the northeast, pounding inland from the cold, arctic heart of everlasting winter. On the flat, unbroken surface of the tundra, we huddled deep in our bivouac sacks and sleeping bags behind what little protection was offered by a small nylon fly pitched as windbreak. We were warm enough but only a few degrees away from significant discomfort. To ease a hike across the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge from the Hulahula River to the village of Kaktovik on Barter Island, Jim Jager, Richard Murphy and I had kept gear to the absolute minimum. title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Trail parking lot break-ins rising SMASH, GRAB: Thieves hit in a hurry even in busy areas, few are ever captured. By PETER PORCO Anchorage Daily News Published: November 1, 2005 Last Modified: November 1, 2005 at 04:06 AM A city recreation official is warning local park users to be alert for smash-and-grab thieves who break into parked cars at trail heads, particularly in Far North Bicentennial Park. "There has been an increasing rash of trail head vandalism and smash-and-bash acts," municipal recreation superintendent John McCleary wrote last week in an e-mail message to members of city, state and federal agencies and to others. "The acts are occurring not just in the dark hours but midday," McCleary wrote. In a telephone interview Monday, McCleary said, "At this point, it does seem that …

title: Hunters show interest in bear refuges
summary: HOMER -- Rod Arno remembers guiding bear hunters in the country south of Kamishak Bay. It was the only place in his career where he led a client to a coastal brown bear and then, the next day, returned with another client to shoot a second trophy feeding on the unsalvaged remains of the first one. That state land around the Douglas River was closed to hunting 20 years ago because a land trade with the National Park Service was under discussion. But the trade never took place, and now the state Board of Game, which begins its spring meeting today, is considering opening the area again. Arno, a hunting activist for the Alaska Outdoor Council, likes that idea. He's even more enthusiastic about reopening a state game refuge farther north, where bear hunting was closed in 1995. One reason for t…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Do beads make fishing too easy? Published: September 4, 2005 Last Modified: September 4, 2005 at 04:51 AM Over the past few years, the use of beads to catch fish has boiled into a discussion among anglers. The beads, plastic imitations of salmon eggs positioned above bare hooks, are deadly effective. Critics argue that little skill is needed to catch lots of fish. It's like, some say, having a laser-guided golf ball. "Who cares?" advocates respond. Isn't catching fish the whole point of angling? If fish populations aren't in trouble, why not make the catching as easy as possible, so more people can enjoy it? Below are the pro and con arguments. You decide. related more Click on photo to enlarge What's in a bead? Just an angler out for a good time Fishing with beads is akin to laser-guided …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Southeast Alaska: Off-the-roadway fishing at its finest By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: April 21, 2005 Last Modified: May 23, 2005 at 04:30 PM I discovered Southeast Alaska as a fly-fisherman many years ago while searching for steelhead on Prince of Wales Island. A friend and I had paid a Wrangell bush pilot to drop us off at a U.S. Forest Service cabin we'd reserved on a remote lake and stream system. For three late-April days we explored the country and its water, casting for steelhead in the stream and learning what we could about the region. Along the way we encountered rufous hummingbirds no larger than a man's thumb and old-growth forests with tree trunks the size of living rooms. Black bears and mink ran the stream banks, and the bones of salmon from the previous summer…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Red-hot Russian opens sanctuary early Lots of fish opens the sanctuary month early KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: June 16, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 07:21 AM Click to enlarge RUSSIAN RIVER -- Red salmon surging into the Russian River in huge numbers have prompted fisheries managers to open the downstream sanctuary to fishing beginning 6 a.m. today. Even better, biologists believe a strong run of Russian River-bound reds is poised to provide excellent angling for at least two weeks. None of which would have surprised the anglers -- or bears -- who formed a relatively light turnout here Monday morning. "The reds are definitely in," said first-time Alaska angler Tim Ott of Sonoma, Calif., as he stood over his limit of dime-bright salmon in the Russian River Campground's Gra…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Tok grizzlie predators fail to become state's prey CONTROL EFFORT FAILS: Moose beware -- bait-using hunters bag only 2 grizzlies. The Associated Press Published: July 19, 2005 Last Modified: July 19, 2005 at 02:41 AM FAIRBANKS -- Hunters took only two of the 81 grizzly bears targeted in Alaska's first predator-control program aimed at grizzlies. The state Department of Fish and Game issued 111 permits for the targeted area in Game Management Unit 20E near Tok. Of those permit holders, 43 registered a total of 70 bait stations. Permit holders were allowed to register up to two stations apiece. Fish and Game officials aren't sure how many permit holders actually hunted or put out bait. "If we issued 111 permits and only three people went out and hunted and two bears were taken, that's a pret…

summary: Outdoors hot line Published: September 11, 2005 Last Modified: September 11, 2005 at 08:14 AM BOATING Push off for Seward to see yachts at Sailing Inc. show Sailing Inc.'s annual Seward On-the-Water Boat Show continues today at the Seward Boat Harbor. There will be about 30 new and previously owned sail and power yachts on display, including models of Beneteau USA, Catalina and Island Packet Yachts. Boats will be open for viewing from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. There will be free hot dogs and prizes and free admission. For more information, call 1-907-224-3160 or visit www.sailinginc.com . summary: Climbing partners form unique bonds RELATIONSHIP ON THE ROCKS: Quirky terms and mannerisms will often develop between duos. By BEN PIERCE Bozeman Daily Chronicle Published: August 28, 2005 Last Modified: August 28, 2005 at 12:43 AM BOZEMAN, Mont. -- Derek Evans picked up rock climbing during the summer of 2000 while attending National Outdoor Leadership School in Lander, Wyo. The NOLS instruction focused on proper climbing techniques, safety, communication and choosing an appropriate climbing partner. By 2003, Evans had purchased a harness and was sport climbing and top roping regularly at spots like Practice Rock in Hyalite Canyon. As Evans' abilities progressed, he began seeking more from the sport. Indian Creek, in Manti-La Sal National Forest on the southern edge of Utah's Canyonlands …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Going hiking? Don't forget duct tape Published: August 7, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 06:19 AM TWENTYMILE RIVER -- By the time we returned empty-handed from the futile search for the missing paddle shaft, Jim Jager had reason to smile. Standing on a gravel bar a long day's hike, maybe two, from the Seward Highway, he held in his hands the latest in Alaska paddling exotica: A carbon-fiber, alder-stick, bent-shaft kayak paddle. He had only one question for Jacques Boutet and myself: Did the jury-rigged paddle need more duct tape? Boutet, who'd precipitated this invention by losing half of the two-piece shaft from his four-piece paddle on a hike over Winner Creek Trail from Girdwood, said that it did. By now, hours had been lost scouring a heavily used bear trail -- …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Galena: Preparing kings the Yukon way SMOKEHOUSE: Family passes on its tradition of preserving salmon. By T.C. MITCHELL Anchorage Daily News Published: July 13, 2005 Last Modified: July 13, 2005 at 07:30 AM Right about now, Alyson Esmailka and her five sisters-in-law are cutting, hanging, smoking and canning the last catches of a late run of the rich Yukon River king salmon. Nearby, a visiting elder, maybe Paddy Yaska or the Iron Man, has likely come down to the river to visit and ends up helping out by honing an edge on one of the sisters' fish knives. A corrugated tin smokehouse on the bank of the meandering Yukon has been a family focal point for almost four decades, since Randy Esmailka's family built it in the late '60s. Randy's father, called Sam Billy, had passed on about then. His …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Simple art of tying flies Lucky for those learning to tie flies that Alaska fish prefer less-fussy lures By RON WILMOT Anchorage Daily News Published: March 20, 2005 Last Modified: March 20, 2005 at 06:15 AM They are tiny works of art to be admired, but they have a purpose: to imitate nature and fool fish. And therein lies the appeal of tying feather and hair onto sharpened hooks -- it's particularly satisfying to catch fish with something you created. Tying flies is a Zen experience for some, a fun, calming craft to pass the winter and spark thoughts of warmer days, feeding trout and feisty salmon. "It's a nice thing to do when you're sitting around the house and it's 30 below," said Tom Cloninger of Three Rivers Fly and Tackle shop in Wasilla. "You don't feel so bad about watching TV if …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fortunate fishermen strike rush of surprise red salmon KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: August 18, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 07:28 AM STERLING -- In a surprise surge that delivered all the late-summer fishing action a few lucky anglers could handle, red salmon fresh from Cook Inlet stormed the Kenai River last weekend and early this week by the hundreds of thousands, roiling eddies and bending rods from Bing's Landing upriver to Thompson's Hole and beyond. That was good news for anglers who had space in their freezers or smokehouses for last-minute fillets. Even better, late-season reds continued to bust into the Kenai in big numbers early this week -- 82,645 passed state sonar counters Monday and Tuesday -- and fishing is expected to stay sizzling hot through the weekend.…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fish with an attitude changes a lake LOOKING FOR THAT TOOTHY GRIN KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: September 8, 2005 Last Modified: September 8, 2005 at 04:47 AM The fish surprised me. It struck like a hammer, as northern pike will, but then surged deep and ran powerfully, like a salmon. I held on as it raced parallel to the shore for 20 feet or so before turning 180 degrees and tearing back past me. I kept tension on the line and tried to turn the fish, but it was too strong. In the end, I managed only to brake it slightly. Then, more in character with the species, the pike stopped and shook its head, roiling the water and spitting my hook. From the look and feel of it Monday afternoon, I'm guessing that fish would have weighed five or six pounds -- a pretty good pike for Sand Lake. Al…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Want to be on the water? Slip into flotation device Published: July 31, 2005 Last Modified: October 22, 2005 at 06:15 AM Counterintuitive as it may seem, thrill sports such as white-water rafting, kayaking, even roaring around on jet skis are not death sports. A couple of things have served to remind me of this in recent weeks. One was swimming the brutally cold Hulahula River on Alaska's North Slope. This I did several times, accidentally but without a problem, at mid-month. The fact these swims were painless and non-life threatening can be attributed to two things: a personal flotation device (variously called a "life jacket'' or PFD) and a water-tight dry suit. Both were worn knowing that packrafting in Class III white water means it's easy to get pitched, rolled or sucked…
